coredata.dc:title: Enhancing aqueous zinc-ion energy storage performance with ion-mediating carbon quantum dots-modified separators regulating zinc deposition behavior
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ting
Liu, Penggao
Chen, Xinyue
Guo, Yingying
He, Chunrong
Guo, Jia
Liu, Weifang
Gao, Shasha
Lv, Yan
Liu, Kaiyu
coredata.dc:description: Aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) hold promising applications owing to their safety, cost-effectiveness, and competitive capacity. However, issues such as zinc dendritic formation and side reactions severely impede their practical viability. Utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to modify cheap, tenacious, and highly hydrophilic filter paper (FP) as separator effectively improves the electrochemical performance of zinc ion energy storage systems. The structured arrangement of CQDs redistributes zinc ion transport and induces uniform zinc icon deposition, thereby improving zinc ion reaction kinetics. Moreover, the rich functional groups on the surface of CQDs readily form hydrogen bonds with active H2O, further inhibiting corrosion reactions. With the assistance of CQDs, the FP-CQDs separator maintains high ionic conductivity and a high zinc-ion transference number, ensuring dendrite-free and corrosion-resistant operation with high coulombic efficiency and a prolonged lifespan of 1200 h at 1 mA cm−2. Zn//AC hybrid capacitors incorporating FP-CQDs separator demonstrate superior capacity retention compared to those using FP separator alone, with a high capacity retention after 600 cycles at 1 A/g, while Zn//V2O5 full batteries also exhibit excellent cycling stability. Given these findings, this study presents a new composite separator for advanced aqueous zinc-ion energy storage systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zn anode
Carbon quantum dots
Separator modification
Efficient zinc deposition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterizations and calculations of Zn symmetrical cells, Zn//AC hybrid capacitors and Zn//V2O5 full batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and SAED images of CQDs. (b) full spectrum, (c) C 1s, and (d) O 1s of CQDs high-resolution XPS images. (e) FTIR spectra of FP, CQDs, FP-CQDs-6 and FP-CQDs-14. (f) XRD patterns of FP, CQDs, FP-CQDs-6. (g) FP and (h) FP-CQDs-6 of AFM images. (i) Optical imagery of FP, FP-CQDs-6 and FP-CQDs-14 separators.

(a) Tafel plot at 1 mV/s of FP, FP-CQDs-6 and FP-CQDs-14 separators. (b) LSV curves of SS|FP|Zn, SS|FP-CQDs-6|Zn and SS|FP-CQDs-14|Zn cells and (c) equivalent of tafel slope. (d) CA curves of symmetric cell with FP, FP-CQDs-6 and FP-CQDs-14 separators at an overpotential of −150 mV. (e) EIS of SS//SS with FP, FP-CQDs-6 and FP-CQDs-14 separators. (f) Ionic conductivity of FP, FP-CQDs-6 and FP-CQDs-14 separators. I-t test curves at a constant potential of 10 mV and impedance spectra before and following polarization of (g) FP separator and (h) FP-CQDs-6 separator. (i) Transference number of Zn//Zn symmetric cell with FP, FP-CQDs-6 and FP-CQDs-14 separators.

(a) CV curves of Zn//Zn symmetric cell with various separators. (b) Nyquist plots of Zn//Zn symmetric cells with the FP, FP-CQDs-6 and FP-CQDs-14 separators. (c) Cycling performances at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mA h cm−2 and (d) 5 mA cm−2 and 1 mA h cm−2 of various Zn//Zn symmetric cells. (e) Rate performances of Zn|FP|Zn, Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Zn and Zn|FP-CQDs-14|Zn cells. (f) CV curves of Zn//Cu half cells with the FP, FP-CQDs-6 separators at 1 mV/s. (g) Coulombic efficiencies plots of Zn|FP|Cu and Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Cu half cells at 1 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1 mA h cm−2. Corresponding plating/stripping voltage profiles of Zn//Cu half cells with (h) FP separator, (i) FP-CQDs-6 separator at the 2nd, 10th and 50th cycles.

In situ optical microscope observation of the Zn deposition process with (a) FP separator and (b) FP-CQDs-6 separator. Calculated structures of zinc binding energy on (c) Cellulose separator (represent filter paper separator) and (d) Cellulose separator (represent filter paper separator) modified with carbon quantum dots. Schematic illustration of zinc ion transport and Zn deposition behavior for the (e) FP separator and (f) FP-CQDs-6 separator.

(a) Schematic illustration of Zn//V2O5 full cell device. (b) CV curves of Zn|FP|V2O5 and Zn|FP-CQDs-6|V2O5 cells at 1 mV/s. (c) AC impedance of Zn|FP|V2O5 and Zn|FP-CQDs-6|V2O5 cells. (d) Long-cycle performance of Zn//V2O5 full cells using FP and FP-CQDs-6 separators at 1 A/g. Electrochemical performance of Zn//Ac hybrid capacitors with FP and FP-CQDs-6 separators: (e) Component geometry of Zn//Ac hybrid capacitor. (f) CV curves of Zn//AC with various separators at 1 mV/s. (g) EIS plots of Zn|FP|AC and Zn|FP-CQDs-6|AC capacitors (h) Long-cycle performance of Zn//Ac hybrid capacitors at 1 A/g.
Preparation process of the FP-CQDs-6 separator and illustration graphic of carbon quantum dots speeding up zinc ion transport kinetics.

head.ce:title: Enhancing aqueous zinc-ion energy storage performance with ion-mediating carbon quantum dots-modified separators regulating zinc deposition behavior

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The author’s contribution is the same: Penggao Liu and Ting Wang.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were modified by simple immersion in the cheap, tenacious, and highly hydrophilic filter papers as separators (FP-CQDs). Special structure of FP-CQDs promote the well-homogenized migration and deposition of zinc-ion. Density functional theory (DFT) calculation was used to calculate the binding energy of FP-CQDs separator with H2O, the stronger interaction indicate they can suppress corrosion and mitigating HER on the Zn anode.

Aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) hold promising applications owing to their safety, cost-effectiveness, and competitive capacity. However, issues such as zinc dendritic formation and side reactions severely impede their practical viability. Utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to modify cheap, tenacious, and highly hydrophilic filter paper (FP) as separator effectively improves the electrochemical performance of zinc ion energy storage systems. The structured arrangement of CQDs redistributes zinc ion transport and induces uniform zinc icon deposition, thereby improving zinc ion reaction kinetics. Moreover, the rich functional groups on the surface of CQDs readily form hydrogen bonds with active H2O, further inhibiting corrosion reactions. With the assistance of CQDs, the FP-CQDs separator maintains high ionic conductivity and a high zinc-ion transference number, ensuring dendrite-free and corrosion-resistant operation with high coulombic efficiency and a prolonged lifespan of 1200 h at 1 mA cm−2. Zn//AC hybrid capacitors incorporating FP-CQDs separator demonstrate superior capacity retention compared to those using FP separator alone, with a high capacity retention after 600 cycles at 1 A/g, while Zn//V2O5 full batteries also exhibit excellent cycling stability. Given these findings, this study presents a new composite separator for advanced aqueous zinc-ion energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: As society advances, energy storage and conversion systems shift in the direction of high efficiency, low cost, and environmental friendliness [1,2]. Aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) show positive research value and favorable application prospects owing to abundant reserves, low toxicity, and stable chemical properties of zinc metal, along with its two electron transfer and low redox potential. AZIBs are envisioned as powerful complements to current commercial batteries [3–5]. However, challenges such as uneven zinc ion transport leading to dendrite growth on the zinc anode, and the thermodynamic instability of zinc metal causing hydrogen evolution and corrosion hinder their progress. These issues facilitate capacity attenuation and even short battery circuits, which seriously restricts the further development of AZIBs [6–8].

Efforts to address these challenges have focused on constructing zinc anode with high efficiency and stability. Various strategies, such as interface modification, electrolyte optimization, and separator modification, have been explored to inhibit dendrite growth and hydrogen evolution corrosion [9–13]. While interface modification and electrolyte optimization have been widely developed, yielding considerable electrochemical stability, separator modification, though a cost-effective and simple strategy [14,15], has received less attention and enthusiasm among researchers. Yet, separators play a pivotal role in battery performance by influencing ion transport. Hence, choosing the appropriate separator is crucial for battery functionality [16]. Conventional separators used in AZIBs include glass fiber [17], polymer [18], and cellulose [19]. Among these, glass fiber separators show commendable high-temperature resistance and dendrite inhibition. Unfortunately, their poor mechanical properties and cost limit their large-scale application. Polymer separators boast stable structures, acid and alkali resistance, and strong mechanical properties. However, their inadequate hydrophilicity hinders their application in aqueous electrolytes. In contrast, cellulose separators (e.g., filter paper) reveal excellent hydrophilicity, strong mechanical properties, and affordability, making them an excellent choice for aqueous battery separators [20]. Despite this, the heterogeneity of their structure affects ion transport uniformity, which further delays their application in AZIBs. Optimizing cellulose separator modification to achieve homogenized ion transport and applying it to AZIBs to inhibit zinc dendrites represents a worthy research direction [21,22], further highlighting the cost advantage of AZIBs. Shen et al. employed an infusion and subsequent drying method to load polyvinylidene difluoride on the glass fiber separators surface [23]. This functional membrane homogenizes the distribution of zinc ions at the interface and improves the zinc-ion transport kinetics. Separator modification engineering induces uniform zinc growth and mitigates the accumulation of corrosion byproducts. Furthermore, various other separator modification methods are gradually being studied as effective strategies, such as the use of MXenes [24], chitosan [25], polyaniline [26], metal–organic frameworks [27], and grapheme [28], which effectively homogenize electric field distribution and zinc-ion transport [29]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a type of carbon nanomaterial, offer a simple preparation process, wide availability of raw materials, biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent water solubility, high electron mobility, and facile functionalization [30]. Since their inception, CQDs have emerged as a research hotspot showing promising application prospects across many fields. The rich functional groups on CQDs provide numerous active adsorption sites for zinc ions [31,32]. Therefore, CQDs-modified separators show application potential in zinc-ion batteries, warranting further study.

Herein, we construct a multifunctional, inexpensive filter paper (FP) separator incorporating coal-based CQDs (FP-CQDs). Leveraging the characteristics of CQDs, the FP-CQDs separator provides abundant and uniform ion channels and homogenizes the distribution of interfacial electric field to regulate zinc-ion transport. This uniform zinc deposition contributes to inhibiting zinc dendrite formation. Additionally, the abundant functional groups within the FP-CQDs separator inhibit proton activity in the electrolyte, thus reducing the occurrence of side reactions of the zinc anode. Symmetric Zn//Zn batteries using the FP-CQDs separator demonstrate superior cycling performance at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mA h cm−2, exhibiting 10-fold longer cycling durability compared to symmetric batteries using FP separator alone. Moreover, even at increased current densities, the FP-CQDs separator maintains notable stability. Similarly, the use of FP-CQDs separator exhibits excellent cycle stability and capacity retention in Zn//V2O5 batteries and Zn//Ac hybrid capacitors compared to pure FP separator. This study provides valuable insights into the application of CQDs for FP separator modification.

Challenges such as the accumulation of byproducts, uncontrolled dendrite formation, and gas escape on the anode surface are commonly encountered in traditional Zn-ion cells utilizing FP separator, which seriously affects electrochemical performance, as shown in Scheme 1 , Ⅰ. To overcome these issues, CQDs are introduced to improve separator properties. The CQDs were prepared by an acid-free chemical oxidation method, as detailed in the experimental section, which shows in Scheme 1, Ⅱ. The rich functional groups of CQDs redistribute zinc ions for rapid transport, induce homogeneous zinc-ion deposition, and further inhibit corrosion reactions.

The morphological characteristics of the CQDs were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image reveals a spherical morphology with a particle size range of 2­5 nm (Fig. 1 a, with the high magnification provided in Fig. S1) In addition, the particle size distribution, HR-TEM and zeta potential images of carbon quantum dots are shown in Fig. S2. CQDs exhibit the average particle size is 2.68 ± 0.02139 nm with the standard deviation of 0.49142, and the lattice-fringe spacing of 0.244 nm, consistent with the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [33]. Zeta potential of CQDs was tested by electrophoresis at −16.5 mV indicat a negative charge surface that can easily attract positively charged zinc ions [34]. Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were used to analyze the structure and composition of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, XPS analysis of CQDs reveals signals corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, with the element content detailed in Table S1. Although the nitrogen content is minimal, an enhanced oxygen content indicates an abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups. The C 1s spectrum displays three distinct peaks at 284.2 eV (CC), 285.6 eV (CO), and 288.3 eV (CO) (Fig. 1c) [35], while the O 1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 531.52 eV (CO) and 533.02 eV (CO) (Fig. 1d). The FTIR spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 1e) shows characteristic peaks corresponding to OH and CO at 3315 and 1630 cm−1, respectively [36]. Therefore, the surface of the CQDs is enriched with functional groups such as OH and COOH, which interact with zinc ions, facilitating uniform transport of zinc ions within the separator [37]. Additionally, these functional groups form hydrogen bonds with the H2O in the aqueous electrolyte, thus reducing direct contact between the zinc surface and active H2O at the interface [38]. The intensity of the peaks at OH, CH, CO and CO groups on the surface of the CQDs-modified filter paper separator has changed significantly, which indicates the increase of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the filter paper separator after modification with carbon quantum dots. The structure of CQDs, FP and FP-CQDs-6 separator are further analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 1f). The XRD diffraction peaks of the FP separator at 15.2° and 22.5° are assigned to the (110) and (200) lattice planes of cellulose, respectively [39]. As for the CQDs, the broad peak observed at 23° is attributed to their characteristic peak, indicating an amorphous structure [40]. The FP-CQDs-6 exhibit similar peak positions to FP, albeit with reduced peak intensity and the emergence of small quantum dot peaks. The introduction of CQDs creates valence bonds that reduce the crystalline regions of the FP, aligning with the FTIR data. Moreover, atomic force microscopy images were used to further investigate the roughness of FP and FP-CQDs-6 separators, as shown in Fig. 1g and h. Compared with the jagged surface of FP separator, the FP-CQDs-6 separator is smooth and consistent. SEM images of the original FP and after CQDs treatment separators are provided in Fig. S3, revealing uneven pores in the FP separator, hindering uniform zinc-ion transmission. In contrast, FP-CQDs-6 separator exhibit uniform pores owing to the loading of structurally regular CQDs, facilitating uniform zinc-ion deposition. Although the small size of CQDs limits their visibility in SEM images, we further used the mercury intrusion to clarify the pore size distribution of the separators. As illustrated in Fig. S4 displaying the pore distribution map of the three samples. Compared to the pure filter paper separator, the overall change in the pore size of the FP-CQDs-6 separator is not significant, narrowing the separator pore size in the appropriate range, e.g., at 200–500 nm. In contrast, the pore size of the separator after 14 h immersion changed greatly in this range. We summarised and compared the detailed pore structures of the three separators as shown in Table S2. The FP-CQDs-6 separator increased the pore volume without changing the porosity and average pore size of the separator, which favours uniform ion transport. In contrast, the pore volume, porosity and average pore diameter of the FP-CQDs-14 separator were reduced significantly due to the accumulation of carbon quantum dots. Separator pore blocking is highly detrimental to uniform ion transport. However, the abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of the carbon quantum dots reduce the tortuosity of the FP-CQDs-14 separator, which contributes to the permeability of the electrolyte [41]. Obviously, optical photographs (Fig. 1i) show that as the CQDs load increases, the color of the separator gradually darkens, confirming the successful CQDs modification on the FP separator surface, which is consistent with previous characterizations.

The effect of CQDs on the FP separator in improving the corrosion resistance of the zinc anode is inspected. As shown in Fig. 2 a, linear polarization experiments are conducted in a 2 M ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte. With CQDs modification, the electrode shows diminished corrosion current densities of 14.9 and 8.6 mA cm−2 for FP-CQDs-6 and FP-CQDs-14 separators, respectively, compared to 20.4 mA cm−2 with FP separator. The decreased corrosion current helps inhibit the corrosion reaction on the Zn anode, underscoring the beneficial effect of CQDs in inhibiting anode byproducts. Similarly, XRD analysis of anode byproducts after cycling for 100 h at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mA h cm−2 (Fig. S5) reveals lower content of corrosion products when using FP-CQDs-6 separator compared to FP alone, further illustrating the ability of CQDs to improve Zn anode stability. Hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) occurs concurrently with the corrosion reaction at the Zn anode. Linear sweep voltammetry tests were performed to understand the complexity of HER at the Zn anode (Fig. 2b). Compared with the FP separator, CQDs-treated separator exhibit higher HER overpotentials, and the FP-CQDs-6 separator showing a higher tafel slope of 163.92 mV dec−1 than 117.5 mV dec−1 (FP separator), confirming that CQDs-modified separators successful inhibition of the HER process (Fig. 2c). This phenomenon can be attributed to hydrogen bonding between oxygen-containing functional groups (such as OH and COOH) on the surface of CQDs and activated H2O molecules, which inhibit HER at the electrode/electrolyte interface [42,43]. Moreover, chronoamperometry (CA) was tested to determine the nucleation and subsequent diffusion behavior of zinc ions under a constant overpotential of −150 mV (Fig. 2d). The current density of the Zn|FP|Zn symmetric battery increases continuously beyond 100 s, suggesting random 2D diffusion behavior of ions. Nucleated ions diffuse along the surface in a 2D manner to find the most favorable location for charge transfer. To reduce their surface energy and exposure area, zinc ions tend to aggregate and grow upward as dendrites [44,45]. The current of Zn|FP-CQDs|Zn symmetric batteries increases rapidly and stabilizes within 100 s, transitioning into stable 3D diffusion. This behavior is conducive to forming a uniform deposition layer of zinc anode, as expected, owing to the beneficial effect of CQDs on homogeneous transport, nucleation, and smooth growth of zinc ions. We also tested SEM and XPS of Zn anode with different separators after 50 cycles in the Zn//Zn symmetric cell (Figs. S6 and S7). Obviously, the surface of the Zn anode with FP-CQDs-6 separator is smoother, more homogenous and dendrite-free. In contrast, the corrosion of the Zn anode with FP separator displays more severe and less flat. Moreover, we analysed the chemical state of the Zn anode surface after cycling by XPS. After cycling, the Zn anode surface was oxidised due to the generation of by-products (ZnO) [46]. It is clearly that the surface of zinc anode with FP-CQDs-6 separator is less oxidised and the surface corrosion is lower compared with FP separator. These results further indicate that CQDs-modified improve migration efficiency of zinc ions and promote uniform nucleation, resulting in compact deposition during cycling. The migration characteristics of zinc ions are generally affected by the ionic conductivity of the separator. Symmetric stainless-steel cells were assembled to test the ionic conductivity of FP, FP-CQDs-6, and FP-CQDs-14 separators. As shown in Fig. 2e and f, the ion conductivities of FP, FP-CQDs-6, and FP-CQDs-14 separators are measured as 1.89, 2.81, and 2.64 mS cm−1, respectively. The ionic conductivities of FP-CQDs separator are higher than that of FP, indicating that CQDs modification promotes ion migration within the separator. This increased ionic conductivity promotes uniform deposition of zinc ions, which inhibit the generation of zinc dendrites. The high number and rapid migration of zinc ions effectively reduce the concentration polarization and concentration gradient of zinc ions at the electrode/electrolyte interface, thus inhibiting dendrite growth and reducing corrosion of the zinc anode. To further understand how CQDs regulate zinc ion deposition, the zinc ion migration numbers of different separators were measured using Zn//Zn symmetric cell through amperometric I–t test, as shown in Fig. 2g and h (Fig. S8 shows the test results of FP-CQDs-14 separator). The transference numbers of the cell with FP-CQDs separators (0.78 and 0.41) were superior than the FP separator (0.37) (Fig. 2i). Moreover, the error analysis plots of ionic conductivity and ionic transference number for different separators are shown in Fig. S9. The attraction of CQDs helps alleviate gradient and polarization at the electrode/electrolyte interface, promoting zinc-ions migration [47].

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests of Zn//Zn symmetric cells using FP, FP-CQDs-6, and FP-CQDs-14 separators are shown in Fig. 3 a. The Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Zn symmetric cell using FP-CQDs-6 exhibits a narrower voltage difference between zinc deposition and dissolution reactions compared to the FP separator. This is attributed to the effective improvement of zinc ion transport efficiency via quantum dot modification. Electrochemical impedance is an important parameter that affects the electrochemical performance of the battery. As shown in Fig. 3b, the Zn|FP|Zn symmetric battery reveals the largest impedance among the three symmetric cells, whereas the Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Zn symmetric battery exhibits the lowest impedance. Notably, the introduction of CQDs aids in reducing interface impedance, thus improving the cycling performance of AZIBs. Moreover, the cycling performance of symmetric batteries at different current densities was explored, and the effect of CQDs on zinc anode stability was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 3c, the Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Zn symmetric battery sustains cycling for more than 1200 h with minimal and stable polarizing voltage at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mA h cm−2. In contrast, the Zn|FP|Zn symmetric battery short-circuits after only 80 h of cycling, underscoring how CQDs modification homogenizes zinc-ion transport and inhibits dendrite growth. However, the Zn|FP-CQDs-14|Zn symmetric battery cycles for only 160 h owing to excessive loading of CQDs on the FP separator. This excessive loading affects its pore size, hindering zinc-ion transport and exacerbating dendrite formation. Cycling ability under fast charge conditions was also assessed by increasing current density. (An enlarged view of different separators short-circuited at the same current density is shown in Fig. S10). At 5 mA cm−2 and 1 mA h cm−2, Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Zn can cycle for 240 h, whereas the Zn|FP-CQDs-14|Zn and Zn|FP|Zn cells short-circuit after fewer than 50 cycles (Fig. 3d). This highlights the detrimental impact of either no loading or excessive loading of CQDs on the cycling performance of the zinc anode. At current densities ranging from 0.5 to 8 mA cm−2 and an area capacity of 1 mA h cm−2, the Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Zn symmetric battery exhibits smaller voltage hysteresis and excellent stability (Fig. 3e). More cycling performance and literature comparisons diagrams of Zn//Zn symmetric cells with different current densities are shown in Figs. S11 and S12. The CV curves of the Zn|FP|Cu and Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Cu asymmetric cells are shown in Fig. 3f. Both curves are similar in location and shape, and the distance between points A and B is expressed as the potential difference representing the tendency for uniform zinc ion deposition [48]. Compared with FP separator, the nucleation overpotential of Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Zn decreases by 23.8 mV, indicating that CQD modification helps uniform zinc deposition. Coulombic efficiency (CE) is a critical parameter for assessing the reversibility of zinc plating/stripping and determining the practicality of AZIBs. Zn//Cu asymmetric cells were tested with FP and FP-CQDs-6 separators under conditions of 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mA h cm−2. As shown in Fig. 3g, the Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Cu asymmetric cell maintains a high and stable CE, while the CE of the Zn|FP|Cu asymmetric cell fluctuates to a larger magnitude. The pure FP separator inadequately supports the plating/stripping of zinc ions due to side reactions and zinc dendrite formation. The voltage profiles of the 2nd, 10th, and 50th cycles of Zn|FP|Cu and Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Cu asymmetric cells were selected to analyze their nucleation and growth behavior. The voltage hysteresis of Zn|FP-CQDs-6|Cu (81.9 mV in Fig. 3i) is smaller than that of Zn|FP|Cu (155.6 mV in Fig. 3h), further illustrating that the introduction of CQDs improves the reversibility of the Zn anode, inhibits dendrite formation, and facilitates the formation of a homogeneous plating layer.

The dynamic changes in the electrode interface during the Zn deposition process were observed using in situ optical microscopy, which studies the inhibitory effect of CQDs-modified FP separator on zinc dendrites. As shown in Fig. 4 a, during the initial stages of short-term deposition, slight uneven bulges appear on the zinc surface. As the zinc deposition time increases, these bulges grow and become loose, indicating the formation of Zn dendrites. In practical battery applications, especially under deep charge–discharge or limited cycles, dendrite growth punctures the separator, resulting in battery short circuit failure. The brittleness of Zn dendrites leads to fracture and further accumulation (“dead Zn”) during charging and discharging processes, consequently lowering zinc utilization and reducing cycle CE. However, in a symmetric cell using the FP-CQDs-6 separator (Fig. 4b), Zn ions are uniformly deposited, leading to subsequent compact and flat growth. This means that the FP-CQDs-6 separator promotes normalized transport, uniform nucleation, and dendrite-free growth. The interactions between separators (FP and FP-CQDs-6) and electrolyte species (Zn and H2O) were studied using density functional theory calculations [49,50]. The adsorption structure of FP and FP-CQDs-6 separators, along with charge density analysis, are shown in Fig. 4c and d. Zn2+ preferentially adsorbs to oxygen-containing functional groups on FP separator with a binding energy of −0.313 eV. However, in the presence of CQDs, the adsorption structure changes, and the binding energy increases to −0.510 eV. This is attributed to the high oxygen content of CQDs, and the high electronegativity of oxygen facilitates the well-homogenized migration and deposition of zinc ions [51]. Comparatively, as shown in Figs. S13 and S14, FP-CQDs-6 separator exhibits stronger interactions with H2O in the ZnSO4 electrolyte compared to FP separator. This enhanced interaction with H2O reduces the activity of hydrogen ions, thus suppressing corrosion and mitigating HER on the Zn electrode. A stable zinc surface that inhibits corrosion and dendrite growth during Zn plating/stripping substantially improves CE, effective capacity, and the operational lifespan of the energy storage device. Fig. 4e and f further illustrate Zn ion transport and Zn deposition behavior in FP and FP-CQDs-6 separators.

The practical applicability of CQDs-modified FP separators was further investigated by assembling a Zn//V2O5 full battery using commercial V2O5 as the cathode (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b shows the CV curves of the Zn//V2O5 full battery assembled with FP and FP-CQDs-6 separators, showing two pairs of redox peaks with similar locations and shapes in their CV curves, indicating that no additional oxidation–reduction reaction occurs with the addition of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5c, the Zn//V2O5 battery with FP-CQDs-6 separator exhibits lower impedance compared to that with the FP separator, which enhances its practical application value. The cycling performance of the Zn//V2O5 battery at a current density of 1 A/g is shown in Fig. 5d. The Zn//V2O5 battery with FP separators short-circuited after 90 cycles, while the one with FP-CQDs-6 separators was able to undergo more than 200 cycles, demonstrating a considerable capacity retention rate and maintaining high and stable CE (98.9 %). These results indicate that the addition of CQDs help improve the reversibility and durability of the battery. Simultaneously, Zn//Ac hybrid capacitors were also assembled, as shown in Fig. 5e. The CV curves of the two exhibit similarities (Fig. 5f). CV curves of Zn|FP-CQDs-6|V2O5 and Zn|FP-CQDs-6|AC in the range of 0.2–1.8 V at various scan rates maintain a similar shape as the scanning rate increases (Figs. S15 and S16). The Zn//Ac hybrid capacitor with FP-CQDs-6 separator exhibits a small interface impedance and fast ion diffusion rate, enhancing its rate capability (Fig. 5g). As shown in Fig. 5h, the zinc-ion hybrid capacitors with FP-CQDs-6 separator also demonstrate excellent cycle performance, with the capacity retention of the FP-CQDs-6 separator (77.1 %) considerably higher than that of the FP separator (38.3 %). This suggests that the incorporation of CQDs can also improve the capacity retention ratio of the zinc-ion hybrid capacitor. These results demonstrate the great potential of CQDs-modified FP separator for practical applications.

Herein, the potential of Zn-based energy storage systems is realized by modifying inexpensive FP separator using a simple method involving CQDs. The small size and regular shape of CQDs regulate the transport path of zinc ions, thereby further inhibiting the growth of zinc dendrites. The abundant functional groups on the CQDs’ surface inhibit the HER and corrosion side reactions at the interface by binding with unbound water in the aqueous electrolyte. Owing to these excellent characteristics, compared to a typical FP separator, the designed FP-CQDs-6 separator increases the ionic conductivity from 1.89 to 2.81 mS cm−1, enhances the migration number of zinc ions from 0.37 to 0.78 and reduces the nucleation overpotential of Zn from 155.6 to 81.9 mV. This FP-CQDs-6 separator greatly improves the reversibility of Zn stripping/plating compared to the commonly used FP separator. Thus, the Zn//Zn symmetric cell using the FP-CQDs-6 separator is cycled for more than 1200 h, demonstrating long-term stability and excellent rate capability. Impressively, the Zn//V2O5 battery and Zn//Ac hybrid capacitor show excellent cyclic stability, minimal interface impedance, fast ion transport rate, high rate performance, and favorable reaction kinetics. This study provides a simple and economical method for efficient modification of Zn-based energy storage systems.

Naomaohu coal (Xinjiang Hami), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %, Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), Zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4·7H2O, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), Zn foil (99.99 %, 0.1 mm, Cyber Electrochemical Materials Network), Commercial activated carbon (YEC-8A, Fuzhou Yihuan Carbon Co., Ltd), Vanadium pentoxide (V2O5, Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd), Dialysis bag (MD44, 1000, Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd).

Firstly, Nao Mouhu coal powder was pre-oxidised in a muffle furnace at 240 °C (5 °C min−1 rising temperature). 3 g of oxidized coal powder sonicated in a beaker of 60 ml distilled water, then, 90 mL of hydrogen peroxide (30 %) was added for mixing until the coal powder is evenly dispersed in the solution. The reaction was heated to 80 °C using oil bath for 1 h, after cooled down to the room temperature naturally, mixture solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. To removed large molecular weight substances, centrifuged supernatant was dialyzed in the dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off 1000 Da) for more than 48 h, the suspensoid obtained by further centrifugation is the material.

The morphology was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU8010, Hitachi Ltd). Material distribution characteristics analysed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM and HRTEM, JEM-2100, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Co., Ltd.). Zeta potential test was conducted using Zetapotential analyser (Micromeritics Instruments Corporation). Material composition and structure analyze by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo FisherScientific, ESCALAB250Xi) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker, ATR mode). Material crystallinity analysed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kɑ, λ = 1.5418 Å). Separator pore size distribution condition analysed by Mercury intrusion (Micromeritics). The surface morphologies of the separator were observed by atomic force microscope (AFM, SHIMADZU, SPM-9700HI). Zinc anode was observed to undergo changes during the cycling process using in situ optical microscopy (SCOPE 53M).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterizations and calculations of Zn symmetrical cells, Zn//AC hybrid capacitors and Zn//V2O5 full batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical performance of zinc-ion cells was studied using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenghua, Shanghai, China) at room temperature. EIS measurement was measured with a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 1 MHz.
All the experiments were repeated, and the error was within a small range.

Ting Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Penggao Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation. Xinyue Chen: Data curation. Yingying Guo: Investigation. Chunrong He: Investigation. Jia Guo: Data curation. Weifang Liu: Writing – review & editing. Shasha Gao: Writing – review & editing. Yan Lv: Writing – review & editing. Kaiyu Liu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22208273), and Tianchi Talent Plan of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region, and Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300420387).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.08.175.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Covalent organic framework with 3D ordered channel and multi-functional groups endows Zn anode with superior stability

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spontaneous construction of nucleophilic carbonyl-containing interphase toward ultrastable zinc-metal anodes
